title: The Six Steps
partLabel: Part Two — The Procedure That Keeps You Alive

slides.heading: The Six Steps
slides.subtitle: Part Two — The Procedure That Keeps You Alive

slides.label: Cold Open
slides.text: A breaker panel in a hallway. Someone has taped a handwritten sign next to one of the breakers: "DO NOT TURN ON — Mike working on Line 3." The tape is peeling at one corner.

slides.label: Scene
slides.text: A hand reaches past the sign and flips the breaker to ON. The sign flutters. The hand belongs to someone who didn't read it, or didn't think it applied to them.

slides.heading:
A sign is not a lock.
A sign is a request.
A lock is a fact.
slides.body: And the difference between a request and a fact is the distance between going home tonight and not.

slides.heading:
Six steps.
Six layers of protection.
slides.body: Each step handles something the previous step couldn't. Skip a layer and there's a gap. A gap in this process is a gap in your protection.

slides.heading: Prepare for Shutdown
slides.body: Find the machine-specific lockout procedure. Know what you're dealing with. Notify affected employees. This isn't paperwork — it's your map. Without it, you're guessing.

slides.heading: Equipment Shutdown
slides.body: Use normal stopping procedures. Verify all moving parts have actually stopped — not almost stopped, not slowing down. Stopped. Flywheels coast. Wait for zero.

slides.heading: Energy Isolation
slides.body: Disconnect from every energy source. Electric, pneumatic, hydraulic, mechanical. Turn breakers to OFF. Close valves. Disconnect supply lines. Isolation means no energy can reach the equipment.

slides.heading: Apply Lockout Devices
slides.body: Lock every isolation point. One lock per person working on the equipment. Your lock, your key, your life. Remove only your own locks when your work is complete.

slides.heading: Stored Energy Control
slides.body: Relieve, disconnect, or restrain stored energy. Bleed air pressure. Discharge capacitors. Support suspended components. Release spring tension safely. Stored energy is patient — it waits.

slides.heading: Verify Isolation
slides.body: Test that the equipment cannot be operated. Try to start it using normal procedures. Check for movement, lights, sounds. Verify zero energy state before beginning work.

slides.label: One Person, One Lock
slides.boxText: Each worker applies their own lock and removes only their own lock.
slides.body: Your lock protects you. Someone else's lock protects them. You never remove another person's lock. They never remove yours. This isn't bureaucracy — it's survival.

slides.label: Tags vs. Locks
slides.boxText: Tags inform. Locks prevent. Tags supplement locks — they never replace them.
slides.body: A tag tells you who's working and why. But a tag won't stop someone from flipping a switch. Use both: lock to prevent, tag to inform.

slides.heading:
"I've done this job
a hundred times."
slides.body: Experience creates shortcuts. Familiarity breeds assumption. The worker who skips LOTO is usually the experienced one — the one who knows the machine, knows the work, knows it'll only take five minutes.

slides.heading:
Every accident starts
with "just this once."
slides.body: Just this once I'll skip the lockout. Just this once I'll reach inside while it's running. Just this once I won't wait for the moving parts to stop. Experience makes you confident. Confidence makes you careless.

items.text: Shutdown: Use normal stopping procedures, verify zero motion
Lock: Apply lockout devices, one per person

slides.heading: Module 2 — Key Concepts
items: A lock is a fact, a sign is a request
Six steps create six layers of protection
One person, one lock — your lock protects your life
Tags inform, locks prevent — use both
Every accident starts with 'just this once'

quiz.question: What's the difference between a sign and a lock in LOTO procedures?
options: Signs are permanent, locks are temporary
Signs inform about hazards, locks prevent operation
Signs are for electrical, locks are for mechanical
There is no difference — they serve the same purpose

quiz.question: Why is the 'Prepare for Shutdown' step important?
options: It's required paperwork for legal compliance
It identifies all energy sources and lockout points before work begins
It delays the job to ensure workers are paid more
It's only necessary for new employees

quiz.question: In the 'One Person, One Lock' principle, when can you remove someone else's lock?
options: Never — each person removes only their own lock

quiz.question: According to the module, who is most likely to skip LOTO procedures?
options: New employees who don't know better
Experienced workers who think they know the equipment
Workers who don't have proper training
